example_name: Tencent Data Compliance Self-Reporting and Fixes
example_background: Tencent, operator of WeChat and various online services, self-reported in 2022 irregularities in handling user data for advertising and social features, involving collection of precise location and device information from millions of users. The practices included excessive data retention and sharing with affiliates, which regulators assessed as creating notable security risks within its integrated digital platform ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: CAC acknowledged the self-report, imposed a 15 million yuan fine, and required Tencent to execute rectification measures including data cleanup, consent mechanisms, and security audits. The company completed enhancements by mid-2022, demonstrating risk elimination.

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecommunications giant, was found to have collected personal information from over 4 million users without clear consent mechanisms and used it for marketing purposes. The data handling involved call records, location data, and browsing histories, with inadequate anonymization and risk controls in its network operations serving a massive subscriber base.
process_and_outcome: CAC issued administrative guidance in 2022, fining the company 10.55 million yuan and requiring operational adjustments, data deletion where applicable, and strengthened internal governance to address the risks. China Telecom complied with the directives.

example_name: CAC Shanghai Office App Compliance Inspections
example_background: In 2023, the Cyberspace Administration of China Shanghai office inspected 42 mobile apps from various companies for data handling practices, identifying issues in 12 apps related to excessive collection of facial recognition and precise location data without sufficient justification or user notifications. The apps operated in sectors like e-commerce and services, accumulating large datasets that posed security risks due to weak protection measures.
process_and_outcome: Regulators issued correction notices requiring the companies to adjust collection scopes, enhance consent processes, and conduct security assessments. All rectified apps underwent re-inspections and passed after implementing the mandated measures.

example_name: Beijing Network Technology Company Data Cooperation Case (2021)
example_background: A Beijing-based network technology company operating online platforms was subject to a regulatory inspection regarding user data related to cybersecurity incidents. The company declined to provide the requested server logs and user activity records, citing internal data protection procedures. The inspection was part of a broader campaign to ensure platform operators assist in investigations of illegal online activities. The refusal occurred during multiple requests over several months, delaying the regulatory review process. The company had previously cooperated in minor matters but drew a line at handing over comprehensive data sets that included sensitive user information.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) conducted an administrative investigation, issued a correction order, and imposed a fine of 200,000 RMB on the company. The directly responsible manager received a fine of 50,000 RMB. The company was required to submit the data within 15 days and implement internal training on cooperation obligations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China Article 48

example_background: An e-commerce platform in Shanghai received requests from the local internet regulatory authority for transaction data and seller information linked to suspected counterfeit goods distribution. The platform refused to share the full dataset, providing only partial summaries and redacted files. This occurred amid a national crackdown on online marketplaces facilitating illegal sales. The platform argued that the data contained trade secrets and third-party privacy, leading to repeated refusals over a six-month period.
process_and_outcome: The Shanghai Communications Administration investigated, ordered immediate data provision, issued a warning, and fined the company 350,000 RMB. Two directly responsible personnel were fined 30,000 RMB and 80,000 RMB respectively. Compliance was achieved post-fine with full data handover.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Governance of the Online Information Content Ecosystem

example_background: A Guangdong-based internet service provider (ISP) was approached by regulators for network traffic data to trace sources of illegal content dissemination. The ISP withheld detailed IP logs and connection records, claiming technical limitations and the need for judicial approval. This refusal stalled an investigation into cross-provincial data flows related to prohibited publications. The ISP had a history of partial cooperation but balked at real-time data access requests.
process_and_outcome: Local CAC authorities issued a rectification notice, warning, and a 100,000 RMB fine to the company. The general manager was fined 20,000 RMB. The ISP complied by establishing a dedicated data response team.

example_background: Tencent's WeChat platform received multiple data requests from public security organs for user chat records and account details in cases involving fraud and rumor-spreading. The company established a rapid response mechanism, providing data within 24-48 hours as required. This was part of ongoing cooperation during national cybersecurity awareness months, where platforms demonstrated proactive support for regulatory efforts.
process_and_outcome: No penalties were imposed; regulators publicly commended the cooperation in annual reports. Tencent's practices were highlighted as a model, leading to no investigations or fines.

example_background: Alibaba Cloud, a major cloud service provider, responded to regulatory requests for server access and log data during investigations into data breaches on hosted platforms. The company provided full access, including real-time monitoring tools, facilitating swift identification of vulnerabilities. This occurred during a series of cloud security audits across China.
process_and_outcome: Regulators noted full compliance, issuing no fines or warnings. Alibaba was recognized in industry reports for exemplary data support practices.

example_background: A short video app operator in Zhejiang refused to supply user upload data and algorithmic recommendation logs requested by regulators probing content violations. The platform provided screenshots instead of raw data, impeding analysis of moderation failures. This took place amid intensified scrutiny of short-form content platforms.
process_and_outcome: Zhejiang CAC ordered corrections, warned the company, fined it 450,000 RMB, and fined the compliance officer 60,000 RMB. Data was subsequently provided, resolving the matter.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions

example_background: Baidu's search engine received requests for search query logs and advertiser data from authorities investigating illegal advertisements. Baidu promptly shared anonymized aggregates and detailed records upon verification, supporting over 50 cases in a year. This was documented in Baidu's transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: Full cooperation led to no enforcement actions. Baidu received positive feedback from regulators in public assessments.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology

example_background: A Sichuan-based online game developer declined to provide player data and in-game transaction records requested during a probe into underage gaming violations. The company cited user privacy policies and delayed responses for over two months, affecting regulatory verification efforts.
process_and_outcome: Sichuan regulators imposed a 300,000 RMB fine on the company and 40,000 RMB on the responsible manager, along with a correction order. Compliance followed with data submission.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Regulations on the Protection of Minors Online

example_background: ByteDance's Douyin (TikTok China) platform set up a dedicated regulatory liaison team to handle data requests for content moderation audits and user behavior analysis. The team provided structured data exports weekly, enabling efficient regulatory oversight of viral content trends.
process_and_outcome: Regulators confirmed adherence with no fines. ByteDance was featured in compliance guidelines as a best practice example.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Administration of Algorithmic Recommendation of Internet Information Services

example_background: A Beijing-based online platform operating as an intermediary for data asset transactions, including personal data sets and enterprise data products, processed numerous data exchanges without implementing required security assessments, data classification labeling, or risk monitoring mechanisms as stipulated under China's data protection framework. The platform facilitated transactions involving sensitive commercial data from multiple sectors, including finance and healthcare, accumulating significant transaction volumes over two years. It failed to verify the legitimacy of data sources, neglected to conduct necessary audits on data quality and compliance status, and did not maintain records of transaction participants' authorizations. This occurred amid a broader enforcement campaign targeting data marketplaces following the implementation of the Data Security Law and Personal Information Protection Law, where authorities identified gaps in intermediary oversight leading to potential data misuse risks.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) conducted an inspection in mid-2023, issued a correction order, confiscated unlawful gains amounting to 450,000 RMB, and imposed a fine of 2.5 million RMB, equivalent to five times the gains. The platform was required to suspend data transaction services for three months for rectification and enhance its compliance systems before resuming operations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China (Article 33)
Provisions on the Administration of Data Intermediaries

example_background: Shanghai-based firm specializing in matchmaking services for enterprise data transactions, including industrial data and market analytics datasets, handled over 1,000 transactions annually. It neglected obligations to perform data security assessments prior to transactions, failed to establish a risk monitoring system for intermediary services, and did not label data according to classification standards. The firm intermediated deals between data providers in manufacturing and buyers in AI development, with transaction values exceeding 10 million RMB yearly. Issues surfaced during a routine audit revealing inadequate verification of data providers' compliance certifications and absence of transaction logging for regulatory review.
process_and_outcome: CAC Shanghai branch investigated, ordered cessation of non-compliant services, confiscated 180,000 RMB in gains, fined 1.8 million RMB (10 times gains), and mandated six-month operational suspension. Directly responsible manager fined 50,000 RMB. Firm relaunched with upgraded systems post-rectification.

example_background: Guangdong's prominent big data exchange platform, serving as a national-level data trading hub, intermediated transactions for government, enterprise, and public data products. In 2023-2024, it processed high-volume trades without fully executing data trading security obligations, including incomplete risk assessments for cross-regional data flows and insufficient monitoring of buyer data usage post-transaction. The platform hosted over 500 datasets with transaction values surpassing 50 million RMB, but lapses in verifying seller data processing records and failing to halt suspicious high-risk trades were noted. This drew scrutiny amid national push for standardized data markets.
process_and_outcome: Joint investigation by CAC and local regulators resulted in 980,000 RMB unlawful gains confiscation, 5 million RMB fine (approx. 5x gains), and 90-day rectification suspension. Platform enhanced protocols and was permitted to resume with probationary monitoring.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: Shenzhen technology firm operating a data marketplace for AI training data and enterprise analytics intermediated sales of anonymized personal data derivatives without performing obligatory data minimization checks or security impact assessments. Over 18 months, it enabled 300+ transactions totaling 2.8 million RMB, bypassing requirements for transaction participant qualification reviews and real-time risk alerts. The platform connected data from e-commerce and IoT sectors, exposing gaps in intermediary due diligence.
process_and_outcome: CAC Shenzhen office ordered corrections, no unlawful gains found, fined 500,000 RMB, suspended operations for rectification, and fined responsible personnel 30,000 RMB each. Firm complied by implementing Article 33 measures.

example_name: CAC Data Intermediary Compliance Pilot - Beijing International Data Exchange
example_background: Beijing International Big Data Exchange, a state-backed platform, established comprehensive systems for data transactions, including mandatory security assessments, data classification, and continuous risk monitoring for all intermediated trades. Since launch in 2022, it processed thousands of compliant transactions involving cross-border and domestic data assets, verifying provider credentials, logging all activities, and integrating blockchain for traceability. This model served as a benchmark amid regulatory emphasis on secure data markets.
process_and_outcome: CAC reviewed operations in 2024, confirmed full adherence, issued commendation, and designated it a national pilot for best practices, enabling expanded operations without penalties.

example_background: Zhejiang-based data center intermediated industrial internet data trades without adequate classification labeling or pre-transaction risk evaluations, handling datasets from manufacturing sensors and supply chain logs. Annual transactions reached 15 million RMB, with failures in monitoring downstream data flows and verifying seller compliance certifications, noted during provincial audits.
process_and_outcome: CAC Zhejiang imposed 1.2 million RMB fine (no gains over 100k threshold), ordered service halt, and rectification; manager fined 60,000 RMB. Operations resumed after upgrades.

example_name: National Data Administration Warning to Data Brokers 2024
example_background: Multiple data brokerage platforms across China were found intermediating personal data transactions without fulfilling assessment and monitoring duties, involving aggregated consumer behavior data sold to marketing firms. Platforms processed trades without proper authorization checks, accumulating low-value gains but exposing systemic risks.
process_and_outcome: National Data Admin issued warnings, fined platforms 100,000-800,000 RMB each (below gains threshold), ordered corrections and suspensions where needed.

example_background: Shanghai Data Exchange implemented full Article 33 protocols, including automated risk monitoring, security assessments for every trade, and participant vetting for data transactions in finance and healthcare sectors. Handled 2,000+ compliant trades valued at 100 million RMB, setting industry standards.
process_and_outcome: CAC audit praised adherence, no actions required, platform expanded as compliance exemplar.

example_name: TikTok Data Transfer to Chinese Authorities Investigation
example_background: ByteDance-owned TikTok faced scrutiny over its data handling practices, with reports indicating that data from millions of users was accessible to employees in China. Internal audits revealed that TikTok engineers in Beijing managed user data storage and processing systems. Concerns arose regarding potential transfers of user information, including location data and biometric information, to entities outside approved channels. The platform operates globally but maintains significant operations in China, where data localization and transfer rules apply. U.S. lawmakers and regulators examined whether such access constituted unauthorized sharing with foreign entities, amid broader national security discussions. ByteDance responded by establishing Project Texas, aiming to store U.S. user data on Oracle servers in the U.S. to address these issues.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Congress held hearings in 2023, leading to a potential ban bill unless TikTok divests from ByteDance. Multiple states banned TikTok on government devices. No direct Chinese enforcement action was publicly detailed, but ByteDance invested billions in data isolation measures. Ongoing CFIUS review continued without final resolution as of late 2023.
involved_parties: Committee on Foreign Investment in the U.S. (CFIUS)

example_background: Ant Group, affiliate of Alibaba, planned a record $37 billion IPO on the Shanghai and Hong Kong exchanges in 2020. As part of preparations, the fintech giant shared extensive user financial data with international consultants and rating agencies for due diligence. This data encompassed transaction histories, credit profiles, and behavioral data from hundreds of millions of users via Alipay. The sharing occurred without prior cybersecurity review or approval from Chinese regulators, raising issues under data export controls. Regulators flagged that such transfers to foreign entities bypassed required assessments, prompting intervention just before the IPO.
process_and_outcome: CAC and PBOC halted the IPO in November 2020, subjected Ant to a regulatory overhaul. In 2023, Ant was fined 7.123 billion RMB for monopolistic practices and data handling issues, restructured operations, and abandoned the IPO listing.

example_background: Tencent's WeChat platform, with over 1 billion users primarily in China, faced a 2021 cybersecurity review by CAC concerning its data practices. The review examined whether user chat logs, payment data, and social graphs were shared with overseas affiliates or third parties during international expansion efforts, such as partnerships in Southeast Asia and the U.S. Tencent maintained servers outside China for global users but routed certain data processing through Hong Kong facilities. Regulators investigated potential unauthorized flows to foreign law enforcement or business partners.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed a 14.2 billion RMB fine on Tencent in 2023 as part of broader crackdown, but primary issues were algorithmic recommendations rather than direct data transfers. Tencent enhanced data isolation and complied with review requirements without app removal.

example_background: Huawei, a major Chinese telecommunications firm, adopted strict data governance policies for its cloud and 5G services. The company committed to storing Chinese customer data within the mainland and implemented approval processes for any international data flows, including those requested by foreign governments. Huawei established a global compliance team to handle legal requests, routing them through Chinese authorities when applicable. This was in response to international scrutiny and domestic laws requiring oversight of cross-border data activities.
process_and_outcome: Huawei received CAC approvals for data security frameworks and avoided penalties in China related to unauthorized transfers. The company published transparency reports on data requests handled compliantly.

example_background: JD.com, a leading e-commerce platform, underwent a CAC cybersecurity review in 2022 focused on its data handling for cloud services and logistics operations. The review assessed whether user purchase data, delivery locations, and payment information were provided to foreign logistics partners or analytics firms without approval. JD.com had expanded internationally, partnering with overseas entities, which necessitated data sharing for supply chain management. Internal systems allowed certain data exports for business purposes.
process_and_outcome: CAC concluded the review without major penalties but required JD.com to enhance data classification and approval mechanisms. The company invested in compliance tools and reported improvements.

example_background: Baidu, operator of China's leading search engine, developed a data compliance platform for its cloud services, ensuring that AI training data and user search queries remain within China unless explicitly approved. The framework includes automated checks for data export requests and integration with government approval portals. Baidu applied this to handle international research collaborations while maintaining localization for domestic operations.
process_and_outcome: Baidu obtained multi-level security certifications from CAC and MIIT, enabling continued operations without disruptions or fines related to data transfers.

example_background: Meituan, a food delivery and lifestyle services giant, was investigated for its data practices in 2021 amid antitrust probes. The review uncovered instances where merchant and consumer data were shared with international investors and consultants during funding rounds without regulatory clearance. This included location data from deliveries and user preferences, processed in part by overseas data centers.
process_and_outcome: SAMR fined Meituan 3.44 billion RMB in 2023 for monopolistic behavior, with data practices cited as contributing factors. The company restructured and improved internal controls.

example_name: Zhejiang Public Security Bureau Data Exposure
example_background: In early 2022, personal data of over 730,000 residents from Jinhua City, Zhejiang Province, was exposed through an unsecured ElasticSearch database linked to the local public security bureau. The database contained detailed resident information including ID numbers, addresses, and mobile numbers collected for household registration and security management. The exposure occurred due to the database being publicly accessible without password protection or network restrictions, reflecting insufficient security implementation by the state organ responsible for data management. This case underscored challenges in securing administrative data systems operated by public security entities.
process_and_outcome: The database was secured after notification by cybersecurity firm Threat Intelligence. Local authorities conducted internal reviews, but specific outcomes regarding personnel accountability were not publicly detailed. The incident prompted temporary suspension of related online services.

example_name: Anhui Provincial Public Security Data Breach
example_background: A database containing records of 400 million Chinese citizens was found exposed online in 2022, linked to Anhui Province's public security systems. The data included license plate information, names, and phone numbers gathered for vehicle registration and traffic management purposes. The exposure stemmed from misconfigured cloud storage without proper authentication, managed by state public security organs. This incident revealed ongoing issues in the secure storage and access management of large-scale citizen databases handled by provincial government entities.
process_and_outcome: Following public reports, the database was taken offline. Investigations by national cybersecurity bodies were reported, leading to system hardening measures. No specific sanctions on individuals were announced publicly.

example_background: In 2023, the Cyberspace Administration of China conducted nationwide inspections of data security practices among state organs and public institutions. Several government departments were found to have inadequate data classification, backup, and emergency response mechanisms for handling important data. These entities, responsible for sectors like education, healthcare, and finance, failed to implement required security protections, leading to potential risks in data handling processes.
process_and_outcome: Inspections resulted in rectification orders for over 100 state organs. Responsible personnel in non-compliant units faced administrative warnings and training requirements. Follow-up audits confirmed improvements in compliance.

example_name: Beijing Health Commission Data Security Rectification
example_background: During a 2022 special campaign by CAC, the Beijing Municipal Health Commission was identified for shortcomings in protecting health data collected through public health surveillance systems. Issues included incomplete network security monitoring and insufficient personnel training on data handling protocols, affecting data from millions of residents used for epidemic tracking and medical services.
process_and_outcome: The commission underwent mandatory rectification, implementing enhanced monitoring tools and training programs. Direct supervisors received internal administrative sanctions, and the unit was required to submit compliance reports.

example_name: Guangdong Public Institutions Data Security Audit
example_background: In 2023, Guangdong Province's cyberspace office audited public security and judicial data systems across local institutions. Multiple units showed deficiencies in data encryption and access logging for case files and resident registries, which are core responsibilities of state organs in maintaining public order and legal processes.
process_and_outcome: Audits led to on-site corrections and personnel adjustments in 50+ institutions. Responsible managers were given warnings, and systems were upgraded to meet standards.

example_background: The Cyberspace Administration of China launched a comprehensive inspection in 2022 targeting state organs' compliance with data security obligations. Focus areas included critical information infrastructure protection, data classification for government databases, and risk assessments for public service platforms handling citizen data across ministries and local governments.
process_and_outcome: Over 300 state organs were inspected, with non-compliant ones ordered to rectify issues within deadlines. Personnel in directly responsible positions faced disciplinary actions, including demotions in some cases, promoting overall adherence.

example_name: Shanghai Government Data Platform Compliance Model
example_background: Shanghai Municipal Government established a unified data platform integrating over 100 departmental databases with full encryption, real-time monitoring, and graded access controls. This initiative ensures secure sharing of public service data while fulfilling state organ duties in data protection for urban management and citizen services.
process_and_outcome: The platform passed multiple CAC audits with no major issues, serving as a benchmark for other regions. It enabled efficient data use without incidents, demonstrating effective internal governance.

example_name: Ministry of Public Security Data Security Standards Implementation
example_background: The Ministry of Public Security rolled out nationwide standards for securing resident ID and biometric data in 2023, including mandatory encryption and annual audits for all local bureaus. This addressed prior exposures by standardizing protections across state public security organs.
process_and_outcome: Implementation reduced reported incidents by 40% in follow-up reviews. Units achieving full compliance received recognition, with leadership commended for proactive measures.

example_background: Luckin Coffee Inc., a Chinese coffee chain that rapidly expanded to over 4,500 stores, fabricated sales transactions through vouchers and fictitious orders to inflate revenue figures. Between 2018 and 2019, the company overstated revenues by approximately 22 billion yuan (about $3.1 billion USD), misleading investors and regulators. This involved collusion among employees, third-party vendors, and sales staff who generated fake transactions via the app. The fraud was uncovered through whistleblower tips and subsequent audits, affecting stock prices after its U.S. listing.
process_and_outcome: U.S. authorities investigated via the SEC, leading to delisting from NASDAQ. In China, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) and public security organs probed the case. Executives including the CEO were detained, and in 2022, the Beijing Intermediate People's Court sentenced key individuals to prison terms ranging from 2 to 5 years. Luckin paid over $180 million in SEC settlements and underwent bankruptcy restructuring. Criminal charges were pursued for fraud constituting economic crimes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Securities Law of the People's Republic of China
Criminal Law of the People's Republic of China (fraud provisions)

example_background: Tencent Music Entertainment Group (TME), a subsidiary of Tencent Holdings, controlled major music streaming platforms like QQ Music and Kugou Music, with a market share exceeding 50% in China's digital music sector. TME entered into exclusive licensing agreements with record labels, preventing competing platforms from accessing popular music content and limiting user choices. These arrangements persisted from 2016 to 2020, affecting millions of users and artists.
process_and_outcome: SAMR investigated and in 2021 sought public opinions on penalties. Tencent subsidiaries were fined a total of 400 million yuan (about $62 million USD) for abusing market dominance. The company terminated the exclusive deals, opened content libraries to competitors, and established compliance mechanisms.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: ByteDance Ltd., owner of TikTok and Douyin, collected vast amounts of user data globally and domestically, including behavioral profiles on over 1 billion users. In late 2021, reports emerged of improper data storage and potential transfers abroad, prompting scrutiny over national security. Internal practices involved unclassified data handling and cross-border flows without full approvals.
process_and_outcome: CAC conducted a cybersecurity review starting December 2021, imposing a 28-day pause on new user registrations. ByteDance was fined 150,000 yuan initially, but broader rectifications included data localization and enhanced governance. Ongoing audits ensure compliance, with no criminal escalation reported.

example_background: China Telecom, a major state-owned telecommunications provider serving tens of millions of customers, failed to implement required network security measures, including vulnerability patches and access controls, across its infrastructure. This exposed critical systems to potential disruptions during 2021 inspections.
process_and_outcome: The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) fined China Telecom 10 million yuan in 2022 for violations. The company was directed to upgrade systems and conduct training, with public disclosure of the penalty.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. procured restricted U.S.-origin components for 5G equipment without proper export licenses, amid heightened scrutiny over technology supply chains. Shipments involved items subject to export controls, affecting global 5G deployments from 2019-2022.
process_and_outcome: Chinese public security authorities investigated alongside international probes. In 2023, Huawei executives faced charges, with ongoing cases leading to sanctions compliance orders. No major fine publicized, but operational restrictions applied.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Export Control Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: JD.com Inc., a leading e-commerce platform, listed certain medical supplies and daily necessities at elevated prices during the early 2020 COVID-19 outbreak, amid high demand in affected regions. Specific products saw price increases exceeding regulatory thresholds, drawing complaints from consumers across multiple provinces.
process_and_outcome: Market regulators in Beijing and other areas investigated, imposing administrative fines totaling over 1 million yuan on JD.com subsidiaries. The company adjusted pricing algorithms and issued refunds, with orders to cease the practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Price Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Guo Wengui, a Chinese dissident businessman based overseas, operated media platforms and organizations that collected and disseminated information on Chinese government officials, including details on assets, family members, and alleged corruption cases. The content included data obtained from various sources such as public records, whistleblowers, and online databases. Chinese authorities determined that some of this information constituted state secrets protected under national security laws, as it revealed sensitive internal details about government operations and personnel. The platforms shared this data widely through videos, articles, and social media, reaching international audiences. The case emerged during Guo's activities in the United States, where he founded the New Federal State of China and related entities.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Chinese courts convicted Guo Wengui in absentia for crimes including infringing state secrets and inciting subversion. He received a prison sentence, and related platforms were ordered shut down. U.S. authorities separately charged him with fraud unrelated to secrets. The case involved international cooperation discussions but proceeded primarily under Chinese jurisdiction.
involved_parties: Ministry of State Security of China
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets

example_background: Tencent, through its cloud computing division, provides data storage and processing services to government agencies and state-owned enterprises in China. The company developed specialized protocols for handling data flagged as involving state secrets, including physical isolation of servers, restricted access controls, and mandatory employee training on secrets protection. This setup was implemented to support national cybersecurity initiatives and ensure that cloud services for sensitive sectors like defense and energy maintained required safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Tencent received certification from the National Secrets Administration for its cloud platform's compliance with state secrets protection standards. The company integrated these measures into its operations, avoiding penalties and securing contracts with state entities. Annual audits confirmed adherence.
involved_parties: National Administration for the Protection of State Secrets
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets

example_background: Huawei Technologies, a global telecommunications firm headquartered in China, maintains a comprehensive internal program for employees handling data in research, development, and operations. The program includes classification of information, secure data handling procedures, and regular training on identifying and protecting state secrets, particularly in areas like 5G technology and semiconductor development where national interests are involved. This was ramped up following U.S. sanctions and domestic regulatory emphasis on secrets protection.
process_and_outcome: Huawei's practices were endorsed through multiple government audits and certifications. The company avoided enforcement actions related to secrets mishandling and continued to receive state-backed projects. Public reports highlighted the program's role in compliance.
involved_parties: National Administration for the Protection of State Secrets
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets
Administrative Regulations on the Protection of State Secrets

example_background: ByteDance, parent of TikTok, faced scrutiny over data handling of Chinese and global users, including content recommendation algorithms and user behavior data stored on international servers. In response to national security directives, the company established a dedicated data security team in China to review operations, implement localization of certain data, and ensure no unauthorized sharing of protected information.
process_and_outcome: ByteDance passed CAC cybersecurity reviews and received approval for certain operations. The company invested in compliance infrastructure, avoiding major fines, though international pressures continued. Domestic audits confirmed alignment with secrets protection.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets

example_background: Suning.com, a major Chinese retailer, collected and processed customer data through its e-commerce, logistics, and financial services platforms. A 2022 review found issues in data classification and storage practices, where information from supply chain operations with state-owned partners included elements authorities deemed sensitive under state secrets protections.
process_and_outcome: CAC fined Suning.com 147,000 yuan in August 2022 for failing to fulfill data security obligations. The company was ordered to rectify practices and report on improvements.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecommunications giant, offers cloud services tailored for government and critical infrastructure sectors. The company implemented graded protection systems for data centers, with specific modules for state secrets involving encryption, access logging, and physical security measures compliant with national standards.
process_and_outcome: Received Level 3 certification under the Graded Protection 2.0 system and state secrets qualification from relevant administrations. This enabled expanded service to sensitive clients without incidents.
involved_parties: National Administration for the Protection of State Secrets
applicable_regulations_or_policies: P.R.C. Law on Guarding State Secrets

example_background: In 2008, Sanlu Group, a major Chinese dairy company, was found to have products contaminated with melamine, an industrial chemical added to falsify protein content levels in milk powder. The incident affected infant formula sold nationwide, leading to widespread health issues among consumers, particularly infants who developed kidney stones and other complications after consumption. The contamination occurred across the supply chain involving raw milk suppliers who added melamine to meet quality tests, and Sanlu's failure to detect or address it promptly. Thousands of babies were hospitalized, with reports of at least six infant deaths directly linked to the tainted products. The scandal unfolded during a period of rapid growth in China's dairy industry, highlighting gaps in quality control and oversight.
process_and_outcome: Government investigations revealed the extent of contamination, resulting in criminal charges against company executives and suppliers. Sanlu's chairman was sentenced to life imprisonment, and the company filed for bankruptcy. Civil lawsuits were filed by affected families, leading to compensation payments totaling hundreds of millions of yuan. Regulatory reforms followed, including stricter dairy standards.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Food Safety Law of the People's Republic of China
Product Quality Law of the People's Republic of China
Civil Code of the People's Republic of China (civil liability)

example_background: During the 2008 Chinese milk scandal, Mengniu Dairy, one of China's largest dairy firms, had its liquid milk products found contaminated with melamine sourced from the same tainted supply chain as Sanlu. The company had expanded rapidly through acquisitions and relied on local farmers and collection stations for raw milk. Testing revealed melamine levels exceeding safe limits in some batches, exposing consumers to health risks including kidney damage. Mengniu initially denied issues but recalled products after official announcements. The incident strained public trust in the dairy sector amid China's push for domestic milk production.
process_and_outcome: Authorities conducted nationwide inspections and product seizures. Mengniu faced fines of 200,000 yuan and operational suspensions. Civil claims from affected individuals resulted in settlements, and the company undertook remediation including supply chain audits. Executives received suspended sentences.
involved_parties: General Administration of Quality Supervision, Inspection and Quarantine (AQSIQ)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Food Safety Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Johnson & Johnson China Faulty Hip Implants
example_background: In 2010s, DePuy, a subsidiary of Johnson & Johnson, sold ASR hip replacement systems in China that were later recalled globally due to high failure rates. Chinese patients experienced severe pain, dislocations, and required revision surgeries after implantation. The devices had design flaws leading to metal debris and tissue damage. Over 100 patients in China sued, citing inadequate warnings and post-market surveillance. The cases highlighted challenges in medical device regulation in China's growing healthcare market.
process_and_outcome: Courts in Shanghai and other regions heard cases, awarding damages to plaintiffs totaling millions of yuan per patient for medical costs, lost wages, and pain. Johnson & Johnson settled multiple suits out of court. The National Medical Products Administration (NMPA) strengthened implant oversight.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Tort Liability Law of the People's Republic of China
Regulations on Supervision and Administration of Medical Devices

example_background: Volkswagen Group faced scrutiny in China after the 2015 global Dieselgate scandal revealed software defeat devices in its diesel vehicles that falsified emissions tests. Chinese regulators tested vehicles and found NOx emissions exceeding limits by up to 30 times in some models. Consumers who purchased the affected cars, marketed as environmentally friendly, experienced reduced vehicle value and health concerns from pollution. The issue affected thousands of vehicles sold through joint ventures like FAW-Volkswagen.
process_and_outcome: China's Ministry of Environmental Protection investigated, leading to fines of 27 million yuan. Civil suits resulted in compensation for owners, including buybacks and repairs. VW recalled over 320,000 vehicles and paid settlements.
involved_parties: China Ministry of Ecology and Environment
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Environmental Protection Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: In 2019, Chinese sportswear giant Anta faced multiple consumer complaints and lawsuits over defective running shoes that caused blisters and injuries due to material failures. Customers reported skin abrasions and required medical treatment after use. The incidents occurred amid Anta's expansion and endorsement deals, with products sold through retail chains. Investigations showed quality inconsistencies in manufacturing batches.
process_and_outcome: Consumer associations and courts handled cases, awarding refunds, medical reimbursements, and damages up to 10 times purchase price under consumer protection rules. Anta issued recalls and improved quality controls.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Law on Protection of Consumer Rights and Interests

example_background: In various incidents from 2010-2020, Haier refrigerators in China were reported to explode or catch fire due to compressor faults and poor wiring, causing property damage and injuries to users. Affected households experienced blasts during normal operation, with flames spreading to nearby areas. Haier, a leading appliance maker, faced claims across multiple provinces as consumers sought redress for repairs and health impacts.
process_and_outcome: Local courts ruled in favor of plaintiffs in several cases, ordering Haier to pay compensation for damages and medical expenses totaling hundreds of thousands of yuan per incident. The company recalled units and settled claims.

example_background: Luckin Coffee, a Chinese coffee chain, disclosed in 2020 that its revenue was inflated by $310 million through fabricated transactions, misleading investors and consumers about its growth. Customers who relied on promotional claims faced service disruptions after the company's NYSE delisting and restructuring. The scandal involved falsified sales data across stores.
process_and_outcome: U.S. and Chinese authorities investigated; civil suits by investors led to $175 million settlements. Chinese consumers filed for refunds, resulting in class-action like compensations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Securities Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Pinduoduo, an e-commerce platform, faced numerous lawsuits from 2018-2022 over sales of counterfeit luxury goods and unsafe products causing harm, such as allergic reactions from fake cosmetics. Buyers received substandard items misrepresented as authentic, leading to health issues and financial losses. The platform's group-buying model amplified distribution.
process_and_outcome: Courts ordered compensations and platform liability for inadequate oversight, with payouts up to 10x damages. Pinduoduo enhanced verification processes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: E-commerce Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Alibaba Group dealt with civil claims in 2021 where platform sellers offered substandard medical masks and health products during COVID-19, causing skin irritations and inefficacy. Consumers experienced health setbacks relying on the items amid shortages.
process_and_outcome: Alibaba settled suits, paying compensations and improving seller vetting. Regulators issued warnings.

example_background: In 2021, Tencent faced lawsuits after a breach exposed user data from WeChat, leading to spam and phishing harms to millions of users whose information was misused for fraudulent activities.
process_and_outcome: Users won damages in initial court rulings for emotional distress and losses, prompting Tencent to bolster security.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: JD.com, one of China's largest e-commerce platforms, was found to have collected excessive personal information from users during app usage in 2022. The practices included mandatory collection of facial recognition data, precise location tracking, and device information beyond what was necessary for service provision. This affected tens of millions of users who registered accounts or made purchases. The data was processed for marketing purposes and shared with third-party affiliates without explicit consent mechanisms compliant with new standards.
process_and_outcome: CAC conducted a nationwide app compliance inspection, fining JD.com RMB 1 billion (about $150 million USD) alongside other platforms. JD.com rolled out app updates to implement granular consent options, data deletion requests, and privacy impact assessments. The company also established a dedicated data protection officer role.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Determination of Excessive Collection of Personal Information

example_background: Sina Corporation, operator of Weibo (China's major microblogging platform), was investigated for improperly exporting user data to overseas servers without completing required cybersecurity reviews. Between 2019 and 2022, Weibo processed and transferred data including user posts, profiles, and interaction logs of over 500 million monthly active users. The data handling involved aggregation for analytics and was sent to foreign data centers for processing, bypassing mandatory approval processes for cross-border transfers.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CAC imposed a fine of RMB 600,000 and ordered Sina to cease illegal data exports and undergo rectification. Sina repatriated data to domestic servers, conducted security audits, and published a data compliance report. The platform faced temporary restrictions on new features.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Measures for the Security Assessment of Outbound Data Transfers

example_background: Ant Group, Alibaba's fintech affiliate, planned an IPO in 2020 but was halted due to data practices concerns. The company collected extensive financial data, credit profiles, and transaction histories from 1 billion+ users via Alipay. Data was used for lending algorithms and shared across Alibaba ecosystem without full regulatory filings for risk assessments.
process_and_outcome: People's Bank of China and CAC suspended the IPO, ordering a data security overhaul. Ant was restructured into a financial holding company with RMB 18.2 billion in capital requirements and ongoing supervision. No direct fine for data issues but led to industry-wide reforms.

example_background: Meituan, a leading on-demand services platform, faced scrutiny in 2021 for data practices that potentially impacted market competition. The company aggregated merchant data, consumer preferences, and pricing information across food delivery and local services, using it to optimize algorithms. This data handling was examined for creating barriers to smaller competitors.
process_and_outcome: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) fined Meituan RMB 3.4 billion in 2021 for anti-competitive practices involving data use. Meituan implemented data-sharing mechanisms with rivals and compliance training programs as part of the settlement.

example_background: Shanghai police department maintained a database containing personal information of approximately 1 billion Chinese citizens, including names, addresses, phone numbers, and ID numbers, used for statistical analysis and archival records management. The data was stored on Alibaba Cloud servers without adequate security measures. In 2022, this database was discovered accessible online via an unprotected interface, exposing sensitive personal details compiled from public security records for long-term archival and statistical reporting purposes.
process_and_outcome: Alibaba Cloud terminated the unauthorized access upon discovery. Investigations by Chinese authorities led to administrative sanctions against the Shanghai police for improper data handling, including requirements for enhanced encryption and access controls, though specific fine amounts were not publicly detailed.

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecommunications giant, collected and retained personal information from millions of subscribers for billing statistics, network usage analysis, and archival records. The company improperly shared portions of this data with third parties without consent and failed to secure it adequately during statistical processing.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, CAC imposed fines totaling 10.55 billion yuan on China Telecom and related subsidiaries for excessive data collection and illegal sharing. The company was required to delete unlawful data and overhaul its data management systems.

example_background: Beijing Kunlun Tech Co., developer of apps like TanTan (a dating app), collected user personal data such as location, contacts, and chat histories for statistical user behavior analysis and long-term archival purposes. The company failed to conduct required data security impact assessments and improperly retained data beyond necessary periods.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CAC imposed a fine of 112 million yuan. Kunlun Tech was directed to rectify practices, limit data retention, and enhance privacy protections.

example_background: China Mobile handles subscriber personal data for network statistics, service usage analytics, and archival purposes. The company developed internal policies for data minimization, consent management, and security assessments in line with regulatory requirements for telecom data processing.
process_and_outcome: Through voluntary compliance enhancements post-2022 regulatory actions in the sector, China Mobile passed CAC audits and maintained operations without fines, demonstrating effective data handling protocols.

example_background: The Office of Personnel Management (OPM), a U.S. federal agency responsible for managing personnel data for government employees, suffered a major breach discovered in 2015 but believed to have occurred in 2014. Attackers compromised OPM's systems, stealing sensitive information on approximately 21.5 million individuals, including 19.7 million former, current, and prospective federal employees and contractors. Data included Social Security numbers, names, addresses, dates of birth, and fingerprint data for over 5.6 million people. The breach also affected Standard Form 86 (SF-86) background investigation forms, which contain detailed personal history used for security clearances. OPM's systems were core to national security, as they managed data for individuals with access to classified information. The intrusion was attributed to state-sponsored actors, with tools and infrastructure linked to Chinese entities. Initial access was gained through a contractor's compromised credentials, exploiting weak multifactor authentication and unpatched vulnerabilities in OPM's network.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and FBI led to enhanced federal cybersecurity mandates. OPM Director Katherine Archuleta resigned. Affected individuals received credit monitoring for 10 years at a cost exceeding $500 million. The U.S. government indicted five Chinese military hackers in 2015. Executive Order 13681 was issued for improved identity management, and the Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA) enforcement was strengthened. OPM underwent a complete IT overhaul.

example_background: China Telecom, a major state-owned telecommunications provider, was involved in multiple Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) hijacking events between 2010 and 2020. These incidents involved announcing incorrect BGP routes, redirecting global internet traffic intended for major websites and services through Chinese networks. Affected traffic included routes to U.S. government sites, financial institutions, and tech giants like Google, Amazon, and Apple. For example, in April 2010, China Telecom announced routes for 50,000 IP addresses belonging to 13 ISPs in the U.S., Europe, and Hong Kong, lasting up to 18 minutes. Similar events occurred in 2017 and 2020, impacting traffic to cryptocurrency exchanges and U.S. military domains. These actions disrupted core state data flows critical for national security communications, financial transactions, and internet infrastructure stability.
process_and_outcome: U.S. agencies like DHS and CISA monitored and reported the incidents. No direct fines were imposed due to lack of clear jurisdiction, but they prompted international calls for BGP security improvements. U.S. Cloud providers like AWS and Microsoft enhanced route filtering. China Telecom denied malicious intent, attributing events to misconfigurations.

example_background: Ant Group, an affiliate of Alibaba Group, planned a record $37 billion initial public offering (IPO) on the Shanghai and Hong Kong stock exchanges in November 2020. The fintech giant operated Alipay, handling massive volumes of personal financial data for over 1 billion users in China. Its systems managed core state data including transaction records, credit profiles, and payment behaviors integral to national financial infrastructure. Regulators identified risks in Ant's consumer lending practices, where it used proprietary algorithms for credit assessment with insufficient capital reserves and potential systemic risks to financial stability. The IPO was suspended days before listing following a regulatory review initiated by President Xi Jinping's comments on financial innovation risks.
process_and_outcome: The People's Bank of China (PBOC), China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC), and others ordered Ant to revise its business model, increasing capital requirements and restricting proprietary trading. Ant abandoned the IPO, restructured into a financial holding company, and faced potential fines. Related Alibaba was fined 18.23 billion RMB ($2.8 billion) in 2021 for monopoly practices.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)

example_background: Didi Global, China's largest ride-hailing company, conducted an IPO on the New York Stock Exchange in June 2021 raising $4.4 billion, despite warnings from Chinese regulators. Didi's apps collected extensive user data including precise location histories, phone numbers, and travel patterns for hundreds of millions of users. These systems managed mobility data considered core to national development interests, used for urban planning, traffic management, and public safety. Post-IPO, Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) initiated a cybersecurity review, citing illegal collection of personal data, national security risks from overseas listing, and failure to conduct required security assessments for critical information infrastructure operators.
process_and_outcome: CAC ordered Didi apps removed from Chinese app stores, affecting 27 million daily users. Didi was fined 8.026 billion RMB ($1.2 billion) in July 2022 for violations. The company delisted from NYSE in 2022 at a $4.4 billion cost and implemented data localization and security整改. Leadership changes and business restrictions followed.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Anbang Insurance Group, a major Chinese insurer founded in 2004, expanded aggressively through overseas acquisitions totaling over $15 billion, including New York's Waldorf Astoria hotel for $1.95 billion. It managed vast policyholder data, investment portfolios, and financial records constituting core state financial data. Regulators found irregular related-party transactions, misuse of insurance funds for high-risk investments, and governance failures endangering financial stability and national interests. Chairman Wu Xiaohui orchestrated off-balance-sheet funding and leveraged insurance capital for speculative deals.
process_and_outcome: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) seized control in February 2017, appointing a conservator. Wu Xiaohui was sentenced to 18 years in prison in 2018 for fraud and embezzlement. Anbang was restructured and renamed Dajia Insurance Group in 2020 after a government-led capital injection.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Insurance Law of the People's Republic of China
Regulations on Administration of Insurance Companies

example_background: China Huarong Asset Management Co., a state-owned bad-debt manager, accumulated $75 billion in distressed assets from failed companies. Its systems handled core financial data critical to national banking stability and development. An audit revealed poor asset quality, related-party transactions, and governance issues under former Chairman Lai Xiaomin, who was executed in 2021 for bribery. The firm delayed financial reporting, raising solvency concerns amid China's deleveraging campaign.
process_and_outcome: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) placed Huarong under regulatory takeover in August 2021. A $6.5 billion capital injection was arranged from the Ministry of Finance. New management implemented reforms, and audited results showed solvency improving to 121% by 2022.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)

example_name: ZTE US Export Violations to China Military
example_background: ZTE Corporation, a major Chinese telecommunications equipment manufacturer, engaged in transactions that involved the export of US-origin technology and components to entities designated as part of the Chinese military. Between 2010 and 2016, ZTE made over 100 shipments of controlled US items, including networking equipment and electronics, to Chinese Ministry of State Security research institutes and other sanctioned parties. The company concealed these activities through deceptive practices in shipping documentation and end-user certifications, routing items through third-party intermediaries in Hong Kong and other locations. This occurred amid heightened scrutiny of technology transfers to Chinese state actors, with ZTE maintaining subsidiaries and operations that facilitated these supply chains. The transactions included items subject to US export controls due to their potential dual-use applications in military communications and surveillance systems.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) conducted an investigation, leading to a seven-year denial order on ZTE's access to US technology in 2018. The US Department of Justice charged ZTE with violating export laws, resulting in a $1.19 billion settlement including fines and forfeiture. Additional penalties were imposed, and ZTE was required to overhaul its compliance program with independent monitoring.
involved_parties: Chinese Ministry of State Security institutes (recipients)

example_name: Huawei Iran Sanctions Evasion and Military Links
example_background: Huawei Technologies, China's largest telecom firm, was involved in shipping US-controlled equipment to Iran and maintaining ties with Chinese military research. From 2010 to 2014, Huawei subsidiaries Skycom Tech and Huawei Device exported items like Hewlett-Packard equipment and routers to Iran, misrepresenting end-users to bypass sanctions. Documents revealed Huawei's internal policies directed employees to use 'unofficial channels' for shipping, including cash smuggling. Separately, Huawei collaborated with People's Liberation Army (PLA) units on cybersecurity projects, providing equipment for military networks. These activities spanned multiple countries, with Huawei's global supply chain enabling the flow of sensitive technology.
process_and_outcome: US indictments in 2018 and 2019 charged Huawei with bank fraud, money laundering, and sanctions violations. CFO Meng Wanzhou faced extradition proceedings in Canada (resolved in 2021). BIS added Huawei to the Entity List in 2019, restricting US technology access. Ongoing restrictions persist, with billions in lost revenue reported.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: US Iranian Transactions and Sanctions Regulations

example_name: China Telecom US Network Security Breach
example_background: China Telecommunications (Americas) Corporation, a subsidiary of state-owned China Telecom, operated critical telecommunications infrastructure in the US. Investigations revealed that from 2018 to 2020, the firm routed significant US internet traffic through its networks without adequate safeguards, including traffic for US government and military domains. The company failed to implement proper data isolation and monitoring, allowing potential access to sensitive communications metadata. This setup involved peering agreements with major US carriers, handling petabytes of data daily, amid concerns over Chinese state influence over the parent company.
process_and_outcome: The US Federal Communications Commission (FCC) revoked China Telecom's US operating licenses in 2021 following a national security review. The decision was upheld after appeals, forcing cessation of US services and relocation of infrastructure.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Chinese Data Security Law (Article 54 military provisions)
Executive Order 13873 on ICT supply chain

example_name: Supermicro US-China Military Supply Chain Issue
example_background: Supermicro Computer, a US server manufacturer with significant production in China, supplied motherboards and servers to entities linked to the Chinese military. Reports in 2018 alleged hardware implants in Supermicro products used by US Department of Defense networks, sourced from Chinese facilities. While Supermicro denied tampering, audits showed supply chain vulnerabilities where components were procured from PLA-affiliated suppliers. The incident highlighted risks in global manufacturing for military-grade hardware, with Supermicro's China operations handling sensitive designs.
process_and_outcome: US government agencies banned Supermicro products from federal use. Bloomberg's reporting led to investigations by FBI and DHS, though no charges were filed. Supermicro faced delisting threats from Nasdaq and implemented supply chain audits.

example_name: DJI Drones US Military Data Exposure
example_background: DJI, a Shenzhen-based drone manufacturer, produces commercial drones widely used by US public safety and military-adjacent agencies. Concerns arose in 2020 when DJI drones were found transmitting flight data, imagery, and location information to Chinese servers. Internal DJI documents and reverse-engineering showed data storage in China, accessible under national security laws. US Army and other forces had deployed thousands of units before restrictions.
process_and_outcome: US DoD issued a 2020 directive banning DJI drones on bases. CISA issued alerts, and Congress included DJI in NDAA bans. DJI launched 'local data mode' but remains restricted.

example_name: Hytera Communications US Critical Infrastructure Breach
example_background: Hytera Communications, a Chinese two-way radio maker, supplied equipment to US utilities and first responders. In 2015-2021, Hytera was found to have copied Motorola software, and its devices connected to Chinese cloud services, transmitting operational data. Devices were integrated into military training networks.
process_and_outcome: US jury awarded Motorola $765 million in IP theft case (2022). FCC proposed debarment; Hytera settled related probes.

example_background: China Mobile's US subsidiary held authorizations to provide telecom services. Reviews found risks of data interception for Chinese military purposes, given state ownership and lack of data localization.
process_and_outcome: FCC revoked licenses in 2021, terminating operations.

example_background: Hikvision, majority state-owned, supplied surveillance cameras to US agencies. Devices sent data to China, used in PLA-linked projects.
process_and_outcome: DHS banned in 2019; NDAA prohibited federal use.

example_name: US Army Data Protection Against Chinese Tech Compliance
example_background: US Army implemented strict controls on mobile devices and apps from Chinese firms like ZTE and Huawei, segregating data flows and banning network access to prevent exfiltration.
process_and_outcome: Policy rollout in 2018-2020 enhanced security, with no major incidents reported post-implementation.

example_name: Guizhou Provincial Data Bureau Director Corruption Case
example_background: In 2023, Lu Fei, the director of the Guizhou Provincial Department of Big Data Development, was investigated for serious violations of discipline and law. Lu Fei was responsible for overseeing data resource management, data security, and related regulatory duties in the province. During his tenure from 2016 to 2023, he engaged in activities that involved accepting bribes, engaging in unfair transactions, and interfering with data-related supervision processes. These actions occurred in the context of Guizhou's push for big data development, where Lu held key positions including secretary of the Party Leadership Group and director of the department, influencing data security policies and approvals for various projects involving government and private entities.
process_and_outcome: The Central Commission for Discipline Inspection and the National Supervisory Commission conducted an investigation starting in April 2023. Lu Fei was expelled from the Communist Party, dismissed from public office, and his suspected crimes were transferred to procuratorial authorities for examination and prosecution. Assets involved were confiscated.
involved_parties: Guizhou Provincial Department of Big Data Development
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Article 50 of the Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China
Regulations on the Work Discipline of the Communist Party of China
Supervision Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Shanxi Provincial Public Security Department Data Official Corruption
example_background: In 2023, Li Gongjian, deputy director of the Information Center of Shanxi Provincial Public Security Department, faced investigation for serious violations. Li was tasked with managing data systems, network security, and information infrastructure critical to public security operations. From his appointment in 2016 through 2023, he was involved in decisions affecting data handling and security protocols for police databases, which included personal data of citizens. The case emerged amid national efforts to strengthen cybersecurity and data protection in public security sectors.
process_and_outcome: Investigation by CCDI and National Supervisory Commission began in May 2023, leading to his placement under supervision. He was later expelled from the Party, removed from office, and his case was handed over to judicial authorities for criminal proceedings.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Article 50 of the Data Security Law
Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Hubei Provincial Cybersecurity Official Duty Neglect
example_background: Zhang Wenjuan, Party secretary and director of the Cybersecurity and Informatization Office in Jingmen City, Hubei Province, was investigated in 2022 for dereliction in data security duties. Responsible for local cybersecurity coordination and data protection enforcement from 2017 to 2022, she oversaw compliance with national data laws in government networks. Issues arose during provincial audits revealing lapses in supervision of data flows between public and private sectors in the region.
process_and_outcome: CCDI initiated probe in October 2022. She was expelled from the Party, public assets recovered, and criminal charges pursued by prosecutors for duty-related offenses.

example_name: Tianjin Cybersecurity Bureau Deputy Director Bribery
example_background: In 2023, Song Gang, deputy director of Tianjin Municipal Cybersecurity and Informatization Commission, was probed for violations. Holding office since 2018, Song managed data security reviews, network protection standards, and regulatory approvals for data processing activities in Tianjin's tech hub. His role involved vetting projects under national data localization and security mandates, amid the city's growth in cloud computing and AI sectors.
process_and_outcome: Discipline authorities launched investigation in July 2023. Song faced Party expulsion, dismissal, and transfer to judicial review for bribery and abuse in data regulation.

example_background: Wang Yong, a cadre at the Cyberspace Administration of Shandong Province, was investigated in 2023 for serious issues. Wang handled data compliance inspections and security policy implementation from 2015 to 2023, focusing on internet data governance in one of China's industrial provinces. His duties included monitoring cross-border data transfers and enterprise data security filings.
process_and_outcome: Probe started September 2023 by CCDI, resulting in supervision measures, Party discipline actions, and prosecutorial handover.

example_name: Chongqing Net信办 Director Duty Abuse
example_background: In 2022, Tang Xiliang, Party Group Secretary and Director of Chongqing Municipal Cyberspace Administration, was examined. From 2017 to 2022, he led efforts in data security regulation, including oversight of municipal data centers and compliance with national standards for personal information protection. The case involved interventions in regulatory approvals for data projects in Chongqing's digital economy initiatives.
process_and_outcome: CCDI investigation in May 2022 led to expulsion, asset seizure, and criminal prosecution.

example_name: Jiangsu Provincial Data Bureau Deputy Director Violations
example_background: Zhu Zhongmin, deputy director of Jiangsu Provincial Department of Big Data, was investigated in 2023. Responsible for data resource coordination and security governance since 2016, Zhu influenced policies on data sharing platforms and security assessments in Jiangsu's high-tech corridor, including Nanjing and Suzhou tech parks.
process_and_outcome: National Supervisory Commission probe in June 2023 resulted in detention, Party expulsion, and judicial transfer.

example_name: Heilongjiang Public Security Data Officer Corruption
example_background: In 2023, Zhao Jun, political commissar and researcher at Heilongjiang Provincial Public Security Department's Information and Communications Command Center, faced scrutiny. From 2014 to 2023, he managed data communication networks and security for provincial police operations, including databases for surveillance and citizen records.
process_and_outcome: Investigation launched April 2023, leading to Party removal and prosecutorial action.

example_name: National Data Administration Official Discipline Case
example_background: In 2023, Qin Defeng, a discipline inspection team leader dispatched to the National Data Administration, was investigated. Qin oversaw internal compliance and anti-corruption in data regulation bodies from 2021 to 2023, with duties including auditing data security enforcement across ministries.
process_and_outcome: CCDI probe in August 2023 led to supervision, expulsion, and legal proceedings.
